meta.originalQuestion: Evaluate architecture options for judge-gym run processing throughput and correctness in Convex. Compare current scheduler+workflow design with alternatives (workflow-lite, scheduler-only, map-reduce counters, queue/table-driven pipeline, and hybrid). Goal: choose the most structurally sound implementation plan for parallel, fast, reliable runs (including concurrent runs on same/different experiments), while avoiding payload size and read-limit failures.

areasOfAnalysis.title: Workflow-Heavy Architecture
areasOfAnalysis.question: Can we keep @convex-dev/workflow as core orchestration and still achieve high throughput and reliability?

areasOfAnalysis.title: Scheduler-Only Architecture
areasOfAnalysis.question: What are tradeoffs of replacing workflow orchestration with Convex scheduler + mutations/actions only?

areasOfAnalysis.title: Map-Reduce Progress Counters
areasOfAnalysis.question: How should stage progress/correctness be tracked incrementally to avoid scan-heavy advancement logic?

areasOfAnalysis.title: Queue/Table-Driven Pipeline
areasOfAnalysis.question: Would explicit queue rows + claim/lease workers improve structural reliability and throughput?

areasOfAnalysis.title: Hybrid Phased Migration
areasOfAnalysis.question: What phased architecture migration minimizes risk while delivering performance gains quickly?

hypothesisConflicts.description: Workflow-first preference conflicts with scheduler-only correctness claims.
hypothesisConflicts.resolution: Deferred to S5 decision gate after instrumentation and phased rollout metrics.

hypothesisConflicts.description: Counter-centric vs queue-centric progress control may create different hotspot profiles.
hypothesisConflicts.resolution: Combine via phased plan: queue partitioning first, then counters with reconciliation/sharding.
